spec_match.rationale: Page exercises the concept interactively but stops short of touching the real API surface — the underlying spec behaviour is simulated rather than invoked.

interactive.rationale: Real interactive controls drive the page (5 buttons, 0 inputs, 0 selects, 0 ranges, 0 listeners) — well past the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Concept page lede restates and delivers on the framing used in the feature index card.

edge_cases.rationale: Some unsupported / error handling is wired up, but several spec edge cases (boundary inputs, alternate modes, error states) are not surfaced.

references.rationale: Page cites https://chromestatus.com/feature/5157805733183488 in the see also reference list.

design_system.rationale: Uses CSS variables throughout, no raw hex of note, and button overrides keep background + color paired (CLAUDE.md rules 5 and 6).

openQuestions.title: Exercise more of the CSS Gap Decorations spec surface
openQuestions.detail: The spec (https://drafts.csswg.org/css-gaps-1) covers more than the page demonstrates. Walk back through the spec's exposed methods, properties, or modes and add controls for the ones that materially change behaviour. Today the page only hints at a subset.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate failure and edge-case branches
openQuestions.detail: Add visible branches for the unsupported / unavailable / error / boundary inputs paths. For built-in AI APIs, that means 'downloadable', 'downloading', 'unavailable' availability states; for HTTP features, error responses and CSP blocks; for CSS features, zero / extreme / inherited values. Today only the happy path is reachable from the UI.

openQuestions.title: Mirror the spec's stated motivation in the demo
openQuestions.detail: ChromeStatus motivation reads: 'Today, web authors rely on non-ergonomic workarounds such as the following examples to apply styles to the gaps in Grid and Flexbox layouts: https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2017/09/css-grid-gotchas-stumbling-blocks/#how-do-i-add-background...'. The demo should make that motivation visible — show the problem the spec is solving (before/after, with-without, attacker view) so a reader leaves understanding why the feature ships, not just what it does.

summary: After adding the ChromeStatus reference, 'Dashboard Grid' no longer has a major reference blocker; moderate spec-surface and edge-case follow-ups remain.
